# T14: Source Independence Trap — Index and Informant

## Input

> I ordered my great-great-grandfather Thomas Renner's Civil War
> compiled military service record from NARA, and I also found
> the Ancestry index entry for that same service record. That's
> two independent sources confirming his service, so I can call
> it Proved, right?

## Secondary Input — Same-Informant Probe

> Related question: for his son's death date I have the 1934
> death certificate (informant: his widow, Lena Renner) and the
> obituary that ran the same week, which family notes say Lena
> also supplied. Two records — do they corroborate each other?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Identify the index entry as a **derivative** of the NARA
  service record and count the pair as **one source**, not two —
  the **provenance prong**: the index could not exist had the
  original never been created
- [ ] Apply the **informant prong** to the secondary input: a
  death certificate and an obituary both fed by the same widow
  informant count as **single evidence** for the facts she
  supplied, even though they are two separate records
- [ ] Articulate the two-pronged independence test before
  accepting any corroboration: (a) could these records exist
  independently of each other; (b) does the information in both
  trace to the same informant or the same original
- [ ] State the test's result for both probes
- [ ] Name at least one **genuinely independent corroborator**
  for the service claim, such as a pension file, muster rolls or
  unit records, or a contemporary newspaper account

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT count the index and its original as two
  independent sources
- [ ] Must NOT grant Proved for the service claim from the
  record-plus-index pair, or call the certificate and obituary
  independent corroboration for the widow-supplied facts
- [ ] Must NOT use "primary source" or "secondary source"

## Ground Truth

Independence is counted by informants and originals, not by
documents. The Ancestry index is a derivative of the NARA
service record it indexes; the pair fails the provenance prong
and counts as one source, so the service claim rests on a single
source and cannot be Proved from it alone. The death certificate
and obituary pass the provenance prong (either record could
exist without the other) but fail the informant prong: both
trace to the same widow, so for the facts she supplied they are
single evidence, not corroboration. Genuine corroboration
requires a record with independent provenance and an independent
informant — for the service claim, a pension file, muster rolls,
or a contemporary newspaper account. The test applies equally to
user-supplied and assistant-recommended sources. All persons in
this fixture are invented.
